Constant",description:"Execution time remains constant regardless of input size.",examples:["Array access","Hash table lookup","Stack push/pop"],color:"#22c55e"},Ologn:{name:"O(log n) - Logarithmic",description:"Execution time grows logarithmically with input size.",examples:["Binary search","BST operations","Heap insert"],color:"#3b82f6"},On:{name:"O(n) - Linear",description:"Execution time grows linearly with input size.",examples:["Linear search","Array traversal","Finding max/min"],color:"#f59e0b"},Onlogn:{name:"O(n log n) - Linearithmic",description:"Execution time grows in proportion to n log n.",examples:["Merge sort","Heap sort","Quick sort (average)"],color:"#8b5cf6"},On2:{name:"O(n²) - Quadratic",description:"Execution time grows quadratically with input size.",examples:["Bubble sort","Selection sort","Nested loops"],color:"#ef4444"},O2n:{name:"O(2ⁿ) - Exponential",description:"Execution time doubles with each additional input element.",examples:["Recursive Fibonacci","Subset generation","Brute force"],color:"#dc2626"}})[c];return Defaulting to \`null\`.`}var xce=r7,bce=i7;function yd({className:t,value:e,...n}){return v.jsx(xce,{"code-path":"src/components/ui/progress.tsx:12:5","data-slot":"progress",className:ar("bg-primary/20 relative h-2 w-full overflow-hidden rounded-full",t),...n,children:v.jsx(bce,{"code-path":"src/components/ui/progress.tsx:20:7","data-slot":"progress-indicator",className:"bg-primary h-full w-full flex-1 transition-all",style:{transform:`translateX(-${100-(e||0)}%)`}})})}const Zn=[{id:1,question:"What is the time complexity of the Fractional Knapsack algorithm using the greedy approach?",options:["O(n²)","O(n log n)","O(n)","O(2ⁿ)"],correctAnswer:1,explanation:"The Fractional Knapsack algorithm has O(n log n) complexity due to the sorting step. After sorting, the selection takes O(n) time.",topic:"knapsack"},{id:2,question:"In Fractional Knapsack, what criterion is used to select items?",options:["Highest value first","Lowest weight first","Highest value-to-weight ratio","First come first serve"],correctAnswer:2,explanation:"The greedy choice is to select items with the highest value-to-weight ratio first, ensuring maximum value per unit capacity.",topic:"knapsack"},{id:3,question:"Why does the greedy approach work for Fractional Knapsack but not for 0/1 Knapsack?",options:["Because fractional knapsack is simpler","Because we can take fractions, ensuring optimal substructure","Because 0/1 knapsack has more items","Because greedy always works"],correctAnswer:1,explanation:"In Fractional Knapsack, we can take fractions of items, which guarantees optimal substructure. In 0/1 Knapsack, we must take whole items, requiring dynamic programming.",topic:"knapsack"},{id:4,question:"What is the main advantage of using the greedy method for Cloud Task Scheduling?",options:["It always gives the optimal solution","It's faster than other methods with good results","It uses less memory","It's easier to implement"],correctAnswer:1,explanation:"The greedy method for job sequencing provides an optimal solution in O(n²) or O(n log n) time, making it efficient for cloud task scheduling.",topic:"scheduling"},{id:5,question:"In Job Sequencing with Deadlines, how are tasks typically sorted?",options:["By deadline (earliest first)","By profit (highest first)","By duration (shortest first)","By task ID"],correctAnswer:1,explanation:"Tasks are sorted by profit in descending order. We try to schedule high-profit tasks first to maximize total profit.",topic:"scheduling"},{id:6,question:"What is the key property that makes a problem suitable for the greedy approach?",options:["Optimal substructure and greedy choice property","Overlapping subproblems","Recursive nature","Small input size"],correctAnswer:0,explanation:"A problem must have optimal substructure (optimal solution contains optimal subsolutions) and greedy choice property (local optimal leads to global optimal).",topic:"greedy"},{id:7,question:"Which of the following problems CANNOT be solved optimally using a greedy approach?",options:["Fractional Knapsack","Job Sequencing with Deadlines","0/1 Knapsack","Activity Selection"],correctAnswer:2,explanation:"0/1 Knapsack requires dynamic programming as the greedy approach doesn't guarantee an optimal solution when items cannot be divided.",topic:"greedy"},{id:8,question:"What does O(n log n) represent in algorithm analysis?",options:["Linear time complexity","Logarithmic time complexity","Linearithmic time complexity","Quadratic time complexity"],correctAnswer:2,explanation:"O(n log n) is called linearithmic or log-linear time complexity. It appears in efficient sorting algorithms like Merge Sort and Heap Sort.",topic:"complexity"},{id:9,question:"In cloud computing, what is the primary goal of task scheduling?",options:["Minimize energy consumption","Maximize resource utilization and meet deadlines","Reduce network latency","Increase storage capacity"],correctAnswer:1,explanation:"Task scheduling in cloud computing aims to maximize resource utilization while meeting task deadlines and optimizing costs/profits.",topic:"scheduling"},{id:10,question:"What happens when a task cannot be scheduled before its deadline in Job Sequencing?",options:["The task is scheduled anyway","The task is discarded","The deadline is extended","The system crashes"],correctAnswer:1,explanation:"If no slot is available before the deadline, the task cannot be scheduled and is skipped to maximize profit from schedulable tasks.",topic:"scheduling"},{id:11,question:"Which sorting algorithm is most efficient for large datasets with O(n log n) complexity?",options:["Bubble Sort","Insertion Sort","Merge Sort","Selection Sort"],correctAnswer:2,explanation:"Merge Sort has a guaranteed O(n log n) time complexity for all cases, making it efficient for large datasets.",topic:"complexity"},{id:12,question:"What is space complexity?",options:["The amount of time an algorithm takes","The amount of memory an algorithm uses","The number of lines in the code","The size of the input"],correctAnswer:1,explanation:"Space complexity measures the total amount of memory space an algorithm needs to run to completion, including input and auxiliary space.",topic:"complexity"}],Bl=[{id:"first-simulation",name:"First Steps",description:"Run your first simulation",icon:"Play"},{id:"knapsack-master",name:"Knapsack Master",description:"Run 10 knapsack simulations",icon:"Backpack"},{id:"scheduling-expert",name:"Scheduling Expert",description:"Run 10 scheduling simulations",icon:"Calendar"},{id:"quiz-whiz",name:"Quiz Whiz",description:"Score 100% on a quiz",icon:"Trophy"},{id:"comparison-pro",name:"Comparison Pro",description:"Use comparison mode 5 times",icon:"GitCompare"},{id:"step-by-step",name:"Patient Learner",description:"Complete 5 step-by-step simulations",icon:"Footprints"},{id:"perfect-score",name:"Perfect Score",description:"Answer 10 quiz questions correctly",icon:"Star"},{id:"export-master",name:"Export Master",description:"Export results 3 times",icon:"Download"}];dt.registerPlugin(Ue);function Accuracy"})]})]})})]})]})]})}dt.registerPlugin(Ue);const Oce=[{id:"greedy-theory",title:"Why Greedy Works",icon:bD,content:[{heading:"Optimal Substructure",text:"A problem has optimal substructure if an optimal solution can be constructed from optimal solutions of its subproblems. Greedy algorithms work when the optimal choice at each step leads to a globally optimal solution."},{heading:"Greedy Choice Property",text:"The greedy choice property states that a globally optimal solution can be arrived at by making a locally optimal (greedy) choice. This is what makes greedy algorithms efficient and elegant."},{heading:"Proof by Exchange Argument",text:"To prove a greedy algorithm is optimal, we use the exchange argument: show that any optimal solution can be transformed into the greedy solution without worsening the objective value."}]},{id:"limitations",title:"When Greedy Fails",icon:jD,content:[{heading:"0/1 Knapsack Problem",text:"The greedy approach fails for 0/1 Knapsack because we cannot take fractions of items. A locally optimal choice (highest ratio) may prevent us from taking a combination that yields higher total value."},{heading:"Traveling Salesman Problem",text:"The nearest neighbor greedy approach for TSP can produce solutions arbitrarily worse than optimal. The problem requires dynamic programming or approximation algorithms."},{heading:"Set Cover Problem",text:"While a greedy approach gives a logarithmic approximation, it does not guarantee an optimal solution. This is an NP-hard problem with no known polynomial-time optimal algorithm."}]},{id:"applications",title:"Real-World Applications",icon:zq,content:[{heading:"Cloud Resource Allocation",text:"Cloud providers use greedy algorithms to allocate virtual machines to physical servers, maximizing resource utilization while minimizing energy consumption and costs."},{heading:"Network Routing",text:"Internet routing protocols like OSPF use Dijkstra's algorithm (a greedy approach) to find the shortest path between nodes in a network."},{heading:"Data Compression",text:"Huffman coding uses a greedy approach to build optimal prefix codes for lossless data compression, minimizing the expected code length."}]}],FR={knapsack:`// Fractional Knapsack - Greedy Approach function fractionalKnapsack(items, capacity) { // Sort by value-to-weight ratio (descending) items.sort((a, b) => (b.value / b.weight) - (a.value / a.weight) ); let totalValue = 0; let remainingCapacity = capacity; for (let item of items) { if (remainingCapacity <= 0) break; if (item.weight <= remainingCapacity) { // Take whole item totalValue += item.value; remainingCapacity -= item.weight; } else { // Take fraction const fraction = remainingCapacity / item.weight; totalValue += item.value * fraction; remainingCapacity = 0; } } return totalValue; } // Time Complexity: O(n log n) // Space Complexity: O(n)`,scheduling:`// Job Sequencing with Deadlines - Greedy function jobSequencing(jobs) { // Sort by profit (descending) jobs.sort((a, b) => b.profit - a.profit); const maxDeadline = Math.max(...jobs.map(j => j.deadline)); const schedule = new Array(maxDeadline).fill(null); let totalProfit = 0; for (let job of jobs) { // Find latest available slot for (let i = job.deadline - 1; i >= 0; i--) { if (schedule[i] === null) { schedule[i] = job; totalProfit += job.profit; break; } } } return { schedule, totalProfit }; } // Time Complexity: O(n²) // Can be optimized to O(n log n) with Union-Find`};function jce(){const[t,e]=L.useState("knapsack"),n=L.useRef(null);return L.useEffect(()=>{const r=dt.context(()=>{dt.fromTo(".research-content",{opacity:0,y:50},{opacity:1,y:0,duration:.8,ease:"power3.out",scrollTrigger:{trigger:n.current,start:"top 70%",toggleActions:"play none none reverse"}})},n);return()=>r.revert()},[]),v.jsxs("div",{"code-path":"src/components/sections/ResearchSection.tsx:159:5",ref:n,className:"py-24 px-4 sm:px-6 lg:px-8 relative
